channel.title: Home on Ritvik Teegavarapu
channel.description: Recent content in Home on Ritvik Teegavarapu

item.title: Normally Distributed
item.description:
There has been some recent discourse regarding the advent of AI, and how this will affect the role of mathematicians in the near future. I think the surface-level aggrandizing that has arisen from people solving Erdős problems with the newest model on the block is far less interesting than it seems, so let us dig a little deeper.

If we are to take LLMs at the face value they are presented to us with, then it must be that a given mathematician has either benefited or been rendered redundant. I note that a hidden presumption in this claim is that a mathematician is, first and foremost, a producer of results. If we accept this premise, we arrive at our current anxiety-ridden discourse fairly mechanically. The whole conversation, however, starts to look strange if we reject it. But before rejecting it, let us follow it to its logical end.

item.title: Bookshelf
item.description:
“What an astonishing thing a book is… Books break the shackles of time.” — Carl Sagan

Reading is an important skill for any academic, but mathematicians are often the subject of jokes regarding their literacy of regular books. As such, I would like to share what books I am currently reading in both the mathematical and casual world! Please feel free to send any recommendations my way.

Academic

Bakry, Gentil, Ledoux
Reading
Dembo, Zeitouni
Reading

Non-Academic

Dostoevsky
Reading

item.title: Presentations
item.description:
If you are at any of these future conferences, please say hello and I would love to chat!

Upcoming

Introducing STACK to Caltech
International Meeting of the STACK Community 2026, University of Nairobi, Nairobi, Kenya (Jul. 27 – 31, 2026)
[Poster] A Blob Method for Kalman-Wasserstein Gradient Flows
Partial Differential Equations for Many Particles Systems, ICERM, Brown University, Providence, RI (Aug. 3 – 7, 2026)

Talks

Gibbs Principles for Markovian Discrete-Time Interacting Particle Systems on Regular Graphs
Southern California Conference for Undergraduate Research, CSU Channel Islands, Camarillo, CA (Nov. 2025)
Gibbs Principles for Markovian Discrete-Time Interacting Particle Systems on Regular Graphs
SURF Seminar Day, Caltech, Pasadena, CA (Oct. 2025)
Towards Convergence Guarantees for Particle Swarm Optimization
Southern California Conference for Undergraduate Research, CSU San Bernardino, San Bernardino, CA (Nov. 2024)
Towards Convergence Guarantees for Particle Swarm Optimization
SURF Seminar Day, Caltech, Pasadena, CA (Aug. 2024)
Incorporating Stochastic Loading into Droplet-Based Digital PCR Model
SURF Seminar Day, Caltech, Pasadena, CA (Oct. 2023)
Classification of Artificial & Real Objects Using Faster R-CNNs
IEEE Symposium Series on Computational Intelligence, Orange County Convention Center, Orlando, FL (Dec. 2021)

Posters

Towards Convergence Guarantees for Particle Swarm Optimization
Bayesian Inverse Problems and UQ Workshop, ICERM, Brown University, Providence, RI (Mar. 2026)
Towards Convergence Guarantees for Particle Swarm Optimization — Best Poster Award
National Conference on Undergraduate Research, David L. Lawrence Convention Center, Pittsburgh, PA (Apr. 2025)
Towards Convergence Guarantees for Particle Swarm Optimization
Southern California Applied Mathematics Symposium (SOCAMS), UC Riverside, Riverside, CA (Apr. 2025)

item.title: Publications
item.description:
(*) denotes equal contribution.

Preprints

A Blob Method for Kalman-Wasserstein Gradient Flows
Ritvik Teegavarapu, Kathrin Hellmuth, Franca Hoffmann, José Antonio Carrillo
In preparation, 2026
Towards Convergence Guarantees for Particle Swarm Optimization
Ritvik Teegavarapu, Dohyeon Kim, Franca Hoffmann
In preparation, 2026

Conference Papers

Classification of Artificial & Real Objects Using Faster R-CNNs
Ritvik Teegavarapu, Debojit Biswas
2021 IEEE Symposium Series on Computational Intelligence, Dec. 2021

Miscellaneous

Introducing STACK to Caltech
Jasmine Bryant, Jinghao Cao, Franca Hoffmann, Jodie Lee, Salvatore Mercuri, Ritvik Teegavarapu
Case Study, Mar. 2025
Competitive Calculus Problems
Ritvik Teegavarapu
Self-Published Book, 2020

item.title: Research
item.description:
“I regard as quite useless the reading of large treatises of pure analysis: too large a number of methods pass at once before the eyes. It is in the works of applications that one must study them; one judges their ability there and one apprises the manner of making use of them.” — J.-L. Lagrange

My research interests lie at the interface of PDEs and probability, with a focus on interacting particle systems (IPS), where collective behavior emerges from local randomness in ways that reflect both the dynamics and the geometry of the underlying interactions.
